splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may adjust assumptions after that date. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability but potentially requiring use of older assumptions.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: The circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, allowing courts to find violations and revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling the term of supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and that courts lack authority to extend punishment beyond what Congress specified.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: The circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s separate requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law. This affects the gatekeeping standard for successive collateral attacks on federal convictions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, meaning they are barred from raising any previously presented claim in addition to satisfying § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. Some circuits have recognized competitive standing for candidates challenging election rules that may affect the outcome, while others have imposed stricter injury-in-fact requirements. The outcome affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting deadlines and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that may affect ballot counting and election outcomes, recognizing competitive injury as sufficient for standing.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election absent a more concrete and particularized injury beyond competitive harm.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Jacobson v. Florida Secretary of State

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions of the Sentencing Guidelines in criminal cases.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while others (including the Third Circuit panel) have held that deference overrides lenity. The split has become more significant after Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary controls over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: The circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. This affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for many drug offenders.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) encompasses inchoate offenses such as conspiracy and attempt, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses like conspiracy to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline or impermissibly expands the text. This affects career-offender designations and significantly impacts sentencing ranges for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions. The Sentencing Commission has since amended the guideline to incorporate the note into the text.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) under Stinson and Kisor, and that inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands the guideline
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning, so conspiracy convictions do not qualify as controlled substance offenses.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the substance is controlled under state law but not under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled or whether any state-controlled substance suffices. This is significant because some states criminalize substances not listed in the federal schedules.

positions.label: No federal-match requirement
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' encompasses state law offenses regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, because the text refers broadly to 'an offense under state law.'

positions.label: Federal-match requirement applies
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, applying a categorical approach that requires a federal-state match.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others hold courts may consider them, especially after the Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6). This affects thousands of federal prisoners serving sentences under outdated mandatory minimums.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant compassionate release, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the changes retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have concluded the opposite based on the statute's structure and legislative history. This affects debtors' ability to retain equity gains when forced to convert due to changed circumstances.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a) and the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors, based on the Bankruptcy Code's structure, policies, and legislative history designed to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must challenge conditions of confinement and BOP decisions about the execution of their sentences (such as First Step Act time-credit calculations) exclusively through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241, or whether they may also bring civil rights claims.
splits.description: The circuits have long disagreed about whether habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy for prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration/manner of imprisonment, versus allowing Bivens actions or other civil rights claims. This longstanding split affects how federal prisoners can challenge BOP decisions on earned time credits, prerelease custody, and similar issues under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration-of-confinement challenges
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's claim challenges the fact or duration of confinement—including BOP decisions on time credits and prerelease custody—habeas corpus under § 2241 is the exclusive federal remedy.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. U.S. Bureau of Prisons

positions.label: Civil rights claims also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims (under Bivens or § 1983) to challenge conditions of confinement or BOP decisions even when the relief sought may affect the duration of confinement, as habeas is not the exclusive remedy for all such challenges.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose punishment for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions at revocation sentencing, or whether revocation sentences may only account for the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit, under its precedent in United States v. Lewis, allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits limit the purpose of revocation sentences to addressing the breach of the court's trust inherent in violating supervised release conditions. This creates significant sentencing disparities.

positions.label: Punishment for violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying the violation of supervised release conditions, not merely for the breach of trust.

positions.label: Revocation sentences address only breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of a revocation sentence is to sanction the breach of trust represented by the violation of supervised release conditions, not to punish the defendant for the underlying conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in criminal cases.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, filed in a criminal case, is governed by the 30-day civil notice-of-appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joins the majority holding that the civil deadline applies because a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that the civil appeal deadline (30 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a)) applies to Hyde Amendment fee orders because they are civil matters ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that the criminal appeal deadline (14 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b)) applies because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether claims of racial discrimination in the making and enforcement of contracts under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 encompass discriminatory treatment in hotels and other public accommodations, and what standards govern such claims.
splits.description: The circuits have applied different legal frameworks to discrimination claims arising in hospitality and business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of § 1981 and its interaction with Title II of the Civil Rights Act. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve disagreements about applicable standards.

positions.label: Broader § 1981 standards apply
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 provides a broad remedy for racial discrimination in the making and enforcement of contracts, including in hospitality contexts, with relatively plaintiff-friendly standards for pleading and proof.

positions.label: Narrower standards or heightened requirements
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims in hospitality settings require more specific showings of discriminatory intent and applies narrower standards to § 1981 claims involving service in public accommodations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers. This split affects the ability of accident victims to recover from brokers who negligently select dangerous carriers.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not regulate the broker's prices, routes, or services but rather impose generally applicable tort duties.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may be used to address claims that could also be raised in habeas proceedings under § 2255, such as claims of legal error in the underlying conviction or sentence. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others categorically exclude them.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider potential legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence as one factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used as a vehicle to challenge the legality of a conviction or sentence, as those claims belong exclusively in habeas proceedings under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, the circuits have divided on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by marijuana users is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and historical tradition. This is increasingly significant as more states legalize marijuana.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical analogue justifying the disarmament of persons who use marijuana.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 FAA exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether workers whose primary duties involve last-mile delivery of goods that have moved in interstate commerce fall within the exemption. This affects whether employers can compel arbitration of disputes with delivery and distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — distribution workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce to their final destinations are 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — distribution workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery or distribution workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' because they are not part of the interstate transportation chain, and the exemption is limited to workers who physically transport goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
